ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301099 represents lemon tea as a general category; the house-made warm variant served at a Singapore restaurant is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0KL covers tea and infusion beverages broadly; no specific code exists for lemon-flavoured hot tea. Closest parent category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34111008 represents tea as a substance; no specific code for lemon tea or house-made warm lemon tea exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 240 ml / 240 g) of warm house-made lemon tea, as typically served in a Singapore café setting.

nutrient_source.title: Lemon Tea (brewed, unsweetened) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for brewed black tea (NDB No. 14355) and fresh lemon juice (NDB No. 09152), combined in proportions typical of a lemon tea preparation (approximately 95% brewed tea, 5% lemon juice, no added sugar baseline). Energy and macros reflect the unsweetened baseline; a lightly sweetened version would add approximately 5–10 kcal per 100 g from sugar. Values represent a reasonable central estimate for a house-made lemon tea served warm.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tea and Tea-based Beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for plain brewed tea
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed tea and lemon tea contain negligible digestible carbohydrates and have an effectively zero glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a numeric GI to plain tea as it contains insufficient carbohydrate to elicit a measurable glycemic response. Per Project Veritas v2 convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value), gi_category is set to 'low', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate content per serving is negligible.

hypertension.guidance: Warm house-made lemon tea contains approximately 1.5 mg sodium per 100 g, making it an excellent low-sodium beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension; however, patients should confirm that no added salt or high-sodium ingredients are used in the house-made preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate and effectively zero glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Warm house-made lemon tea in its unsweetened or lightly sweetened form contains minimal digestible carbohydrate (approximately 0.5 g per 100 g) and exerts a negligible glycemic load, making it a suitable beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; patients should request preparation without added sugar or honey to maintain glycemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of tea polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Warm house-made lemon tea contains no saturated fat or cholesterol and provides tea-derived polyphenols (flavonoids) and vitamin C from lemon juice, which are associated with modest cardioprotective effects; it is a heart-friendly beverage when consumed without excessive added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Warm House-made Lemon Tea is a hot beverage prepared by brewing black tea and combining it with freshly squeezed or concentrated lemon juice, served warm. It is a common café and restaurant offering in Singapore, where it is valued for its refreshing citrus flavour balanced against the mild astringency of brewed tea. The preparation is minimally processed: hot water is used to brew black tea leaves or a tea bag, lemon juice is added, and the drink may be lightly sweetened with sugar or honey according to customer preference. The texture is liquid and smooth, with a bright, tangy, slightly tart flavour profile and a warm, soothing quality. Nutritionally, warm lemon tea is extremely low in calories (approximately 2 kcal per 100 g in its unsweetened form), contains negligible protein and fat, and provides a small amount of carbohydrate primarily from the lemon juice. It contributes modest vitamin C from the lemon component and trace potassium. The glycemic load per serving is effectively zero, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. It is naturally free of allergens, gluten, dairy, and cholesterol. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals with hypertension (very low sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (negligible glycemic impact when unsweetened), and cardiovascular disease (no saturated fat, potential polyphenol benefit). It is widely consumed as a morning or afternoon beverage and pairs well with light meals and snacks.
common_user_queries: Is lemon tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in warm lemon tea?
Can I drink lemon tea if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of lemon tea?
Is warm lemon tea suitable for weight loss?
What are the nutrients in lemon tea?
Is warm lemon tea good for the heart?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for 'Warm House-made Lemon Tea' exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for brewed black tea and fresh lemon juice combined in typical proportions. The house-made preparation method and exact sugar content vary by restaurant, introducing uncertainty. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero as brewed tea and lemon juice contain no meaningful protein at typical dilution levels; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central brewed tea and lemon juice entries. 2. fat_g is naturally zero as this is a tea-based beverage with no fat-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and dietary_fibre_g are confirmed zero for this beverage type per USDA FoodData Central. 4. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per Project Veritas v2 convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 since carbohydrate per serving is negligible. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for brewed tea or lemon juice in the source database at typical dilution. 6. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 7. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 8. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 9. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 10. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as tea and lemon juice contain no vitamin B12; not reported in source. 11. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported at meaningful levels in the source database for this beverage. 12. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 13. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 14. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 15. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 16. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 17. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this beverage. 18. Cuisine tags in the input (Ramen, Pizza, Pasta) appear to be restaurant-level tags from field survey and do not reflect the food item itself; they have been disregarded. 19. Energy_kcal of 2.0 kcal per 100 g reflects the unsweetened baseline; a sweetened version may contribute 5–15 kcal per 100 g depending on sugar added.

Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for protein_g and fat_g (naturally zero, confirmed zero).
Set glycemic_index to 1 per zero-calorie beverage convention; set gi_category to 'low'; set glycemic_load_per_serving to 0.0.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with explicit reasons.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Used only approved ontology match_type values: exact, closest_parent, none.
Used USDA FoodData Central as approved secondary source with adaptation documented.
